vulnerability.title: Enable auditd Service
vulnerability.description: The auditd service is an essential userspace component of the Linux Auditing System, as it is responsible for writing audit records to disk. The auditd service can be enabled with the following command: $ sudo systemctl enable auditd.service
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q audit; }; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'auditd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'auditd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'auditd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: Enabling and starting the auditd service is a standard STIG remediation that does not modify authentication mechanisms, networking, or filesystem layouts. It merely ensures audit logs are collected, posing minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.

GRUB_TIMEOUT=5
GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR="$(sed 's, release .*$,,g' /etc/system-release)"

execution_details.stdout:
# /usr/lib/systemd/system/auditd.service
[Unit]
Description=Security Auditing Service
DefaultDependencies=no
## If auditd is sending or recieving remote logging, copy this file to
## /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service and comment out the first After and
## uncomment the second so that network-online.target is part of After.
## then comment the first Before and uncomment the second Before to remove
## sysinit.target from "Before".
## If using remote logging, ensure that the systemd-update-utmp.service file
## is updated to remove the After=auditd.service directive to prevent a
## boot-time ordering cycle.
After=local-fs.target systemd-tmpfiles-setup.service
##After=network-online.target local-fs.target systemd-tmpfiles-setup.service
Before=sysinit.target shutdown.target
##Before=shutdown.target
Conflicts=shutdown.target
RefuseManualStop=yes
ConditionKernelCommandLine=!audit=0
ConditionKernelCommandLine=!audit=off

Documentation=man:auditd(8) https://github.com/linux-audit/audit-documentation

[Service]
Type=forking
PIDFile=/run/auditd.pid
ExecStart=/sbin/auditd
## To not use augenrules, copy this file to /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service
## and comment/delete the next line and uncomment the auditctl line.
## NOTE: augenrules expect any rules to be added to /etc/audit/rules.d/
ExecStartPost=-/sbin/augenrules --load
#ExecStartPost=-/sbin/auditctl -R /etc/audit/audit.rules
# By default we don't clear the rules on exit. To enable this, uncomment
# the next line after copying the file to /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service
#ExecStopPost=/sbin/auditctl -R /etc/audit/audit-stop.rules
Restart=on-failure
# Do not restart for intentional exits. See EXIT CODES section in auditd(8).
RestartPreventExitStatus=2 4 6

### Security Settings ###
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=true
LockPersonality=true
# The following control prevents rules on /proc so its off by default
#ProtectControlGroups=true
## The following control prevents rules on /usr/lib/modules/ its off by default
#ProtectKernelModules=true
RestrictRealtime=true

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target


# /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d/override.conf
[Service]
RefuseManualStop=no
[Unit]
RefuseManualStop=no

review_feedback: Review+QA approved but the verification scan failed. Try a different approach.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly unmasks, enables, and starts the auditd service, satisfying the OpenSCAP rule. It is safe and idempotent, though there are optional improvements that could make it more streamlined and reduce unnecessary changes.
concerns: Potential for log disk exhaustion if audit rules are overly verbose and log rotation is not configured.
Unmasking a service that was intentionally masked could introduce audit traffic contrary to local policy.
Installing the audit package via dnf may fail on systems without network access or with a different package manager.
suggested_improvements: Use `systemctl enable --now auditd.service` to combine enable and start in a single atomic operation.
Wrap the package installation step in a conditional that checks for network availability or simply omit it if the environment guarantees the package is present.
Add explicit logrotate configuration for /var/log/audit/* to prevent filesystem fill‑up.
Include a check for an existing intentional mask (e.g., a comment or admin flag) and allow the rule to be marked not applicable rather than forcibly unmasking.
Avoid editing auditd.conf unless required by policy; instead, document configuration changes separately.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly enables, unmasks and starts auditd and adds useful safeguards. It resolves the vulnerability, but some steps are overly aggressive or unnecessary for the simple requirement.
concerns: Adds a kernel boot parameter and rebuilds GRUB, requiring a reboot that may be unnecessary for just enabling the service.
Modifies auditd.conf log rotation settings which may not be needed and could conflict with site‑specific policies.
Unconditionally installs the audit package, which could fail on offline systems.
Creates a systemd drop‑in that may interfere with custom hardening profiles that intentionally mask auditd.
suggested_improvements: Limit changes to enabling/unmasking the service and optionally verify the kernel audit subsystem without editing GRUB.
Make the package installation conditional on package presence or network availability.
Document or configure logrotate separately rather than altering auditd.conf directly.
Check for an intentional mask flag before unmasking, and skip remediation if masking is intentional.
